item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read the "explanation", read it again, and once more to be sure. Then looked at the graph. I… couldn't get it. And that's brilliant! You know things like these are obtuse when you go over them over and over and the inner workings of the stuff just fly well over your head. Well done indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Christ, a Ponzi Scheme targeting hell itself is fucking brilliant. I was a tad lost in some of the more jargony bits, but that is to be expected when dealing with a very technical concept such as this, and I find that it added to the charm of the article. I ultimately understood everything that I needed to: this bank makes a deal with a demon and then makes a ton of extra deals such that they do not have to pay the consequences of said deals in any meaningful way (save for the odd homeless Bermudan woman here and there), and the Foundation is fruitlessly trying to keep them from doing anything too stupid. The satire here is clear, and I fucking love it. Good shit, Buttfranklin, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, full disclosure: I didn't read the whole thing. And I couldn't fully make sense of it. But I think I got the gist, and the gist was, a bank is cutting deals with demons to cut more deals with more demons to cut more deals with more demons and overall, at the end of the day, the bank is actually cheating a shit-ton of demons out of souls or whatever the demons' respective asking prices are.

Which is pretty goddamn funny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for your kind words! And I agree with you about the bloat. In hindsight, too many of the references mentioned in my author comment are so obscure and unnecessary that their only benefit is tickling my specific fancy, so they're just fat on the narrative meat that should have been trimmed. The entire "History" section, for instance, could be culled without harming the narrative. I also was a little too exhaustive in some of my descriptions, e.g. of the demons-in-all-but-name. To be honest, it's not just in hindsight that I realise this; I was quite aware before posting that the prose was a bit constipated, but, like a twat, I was too precious to kill the darlings responsible for bunging up the pipes.

I actually noticed those weird full stops after reading your original, unedited comment! According to this page's history, you can attribute them to the mysterious "account deleted", who also went to the liberty of Americanising (/Americanizing) my spelling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this SCP is kind of bloated — but so's the very thing it's satirizing. On one hand, I still think the front-end could be paired down and made into a much more lean, mean, number-crunching machine — on the other hand, the case study, the incident log, and the very premise behind it are all fantastic — and the execution is more than sharp enough to gain my upvote.

(I also loved the bit about the cornmeal and the town of Bielefeld)

ETA: Is there a reason for the periods after Senior Researcher Dell'Avaro's name in the incident log? It happens a few times, and doesn't seem to make much sense (looks like just a cut-and-paste error, maybe?).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bwa bwa! My justification is that I was satirising real-world systems whose extreme complexity is a defining feature. In terms of making it look like the thing it's supposed to look like, making it really complicated was kind of mandatory.

And, I mean, I don't know if everybody's writing about literal Faustian pacts. (Buffy the Vampire Slayer has a bunch of business-minded demons in it, I guess.) Other people in this thread have been kind enough to remark upon the originality of the idea.

Boring in all caps must be boring indeed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wolfram and Hart, is that you?

Jokes aside, it's pretty generic really. The detail in it is great and feels accurate, but the detail being accurate actually makes it MORE bland, by simple nature of business documents are typically very bland. Everyone's got the megacorp(usually law offices, banks also make sense) that literally deals with the devil for whatever, and this doesn't really do anything that feels new with the concept.

Except go into exhaustive detail about the particulars, of course, which again, just makes it worse for me because it's so BORING. Well executed, but nothing notable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is exactly what I have been babbling about re: weird economics, since at least the MTF contest. I am slightly jealous that you got to this particular market first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For such a complex skip, it does have quite a simple and grim outcome: the Foundation will never succeed. The number of oaths will keep growing and sooner or later, the entire system will collapse on itself and we'll get an XK or worse.

Nice meta reasoning as to why this isn't Keter, by the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gives new meaning to "amortization." +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I tried to indicate multiple revisions with "An executive summary of The General Agreement on the Regulation and Maintenance of Reality (2015, version 21)". Whereabouts does it imply that there's been only two? I'll change it to make it clearer

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't see this, my apologies. I was aware that the strikethroughs mean removals, its just that that it's made explicitly clear that there have only been two versions of GARMR and that example was in opposition to that. But if it doesn't affect anyone else's understanding, I don't think it's a problem. And the comment about strikethroughs was mostly a consistency thing even if it isn't strictly necessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope, just a silly mistake. It's meant to refer to the staff defections. Thanks for pointing it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Deal with the devil + investment banking tactics from the global securitisation / derivatives market. That is *such* a good idea that I can't believe I haven't seen it used before.

This is a very solid extrapolation of the idea, too. A few parts of the satire were a bit heavy-handed, and the opening is quite slow to come into focus, but the case study and the incident log are a lot of fun, and the idea of regulatory capture is well-delivered.

One question: in the incident log, is the entry for 2013-01-09 meant to refer to the two previous time entries? It current refers to incidents in 2014, so I spent some time wondering about whether there was a time-travel angle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The strikethroughs represent revisions; it's a common style thing here on the site, but it's also based on a real-world practice for various types of official documentation:

It started at the time when a correction was made by striking something out in a physical document and inserting any corrections between the lines with a ^ mark, but it's been retained in the typerwriter/digital era because gives both an in-document record of changes and can provide context/further clarification of the change being made. For example "widespread" emphasizes the fact they are now shooting for "zero". You can see it frequently in things like national constitutions indicating parts changed or nullified by amendments, and some bank or inventory records indicating both the correction to an error and what that error originally was; both of which can potentially be useful.

as having two edits of the same thing recorded implies that there's a version after the 2010 version but before the 2015 revision

Yes. That's exactly what it means.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love the article, although I have one minor complaint. It's unclear what the strikethroughs in GARMR are meant to represent. It's said that the underlined sections represent additions from the 2010 version, so I feel like it should be explictly said that the strikethroughs are removals. If this is the case, then "animals plants or animals" should be changed to be one or the other, as having two edits of the same thing recorded implies that there's a version after the 2010 version but before the 2015 revision.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bielefeld_Conspiracy

Or is it?

Might be worth moving dates back to coincide with when this theory started.

edit: didn't see WrongJohnSilver posted the same link, oops.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It stands to reason that Foundation personnel will use the same specialized nomenclature as the bank personnel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I second some of TL333s' concerns - namely the Foundation using context-specific jargon which they wouldn't normally use - I like it enough in terms of presentation and concept to upvote.

The bank summons the spirit of Freewater Homes, a Bermuda-based housing association

And that sentence made me laugh, too. "Summoning the spirit" of a housing association. That's some Douglas Adams shit right there. Actually, come to think of it, a lot of this article has the vibe of something one would find in the Hitchhiker's universe, but not in a bad way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I were to look at this anomaly, what would I see? Could I? What's the deal with the bank?

There's nothing physical to see. The really short version is that it's a gargantuan paranormal ponzi scheme created by this bank. They're doing things like using a deal with a demon to pay off the jinn that they had summon a bunch of gold to pay off the leprechaun that they had change the course of that river to pay off the water elemental that…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't follow this at all. I generally don't like anomalies where the Foundation is shown to not only have knowledge and understanding of the general magical/anomalous community at large, but uses the parlance thereof. This isn't science, in other words.

Furthermore, I can't even fathom what this is. If I were to look at this anomaly, what would I see? Could I? What's the deal with the bank? Like, why are they so important in all this? I'm sure some of the answers are in the article, but after a while, I just my time shaking my head and skimming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know just enough about international finance to find this disturbingly plausible. Well plotted, clearly written, more terrifying than any cold-posted rage-stating TWKU.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. Interesting concept, well written, and tons of logs. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My favorite part is all the additional stipulations they've had to add to GARMR. I read them all in the voice of an increasingly exasperated Sheldon Katz, Esq.:
"Okay. From here out, we'll all agree to just strike the 'widespread'."

"OR DUPLICATION."

"You know what, let's just add a new clause here, defining acceptable collateral…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. I'm in finance, so the structure of these deals interests me.

2. This would tickle a friend of mine, who is a professor of network science.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bielefeld

still there

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is so weird and complex that I can't help but love it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I just came from all the magitek. It is so good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If anomalies exist, someone is going to use them to make money.

Faust made a deal with the devil and paid the price. What if you could make a million deals with a million devils — and get away scot-free?

Thanks to AidenEldritch, Shio and 9Volt for giving this a look-over.

Images: I made the "schematic representation" image with the free, open-source program Gephi, visualising its demo data set of, if I recall correctly, a dependency graph for a Java program. I made the "case study" image with Microsoft Publisher.

Because I can’t bear the idea of people not picking up on my countless self-pleasing references:

+ wahoo!
– wahoo!

A watchdog body that’s a LITERAL watchdog of Hell, lmao.

Images like these were the inspiration for the case study in this article.

SPV usually stands for something else.

K2 can stand for this thing.

“STC” is pretty close to “SSC”, an irl project that was meant to discover physical truths about reality, never got completed, and whose would-be researchers ended up working in the finance industry.

The “BBB‒” rating was meant to be an analogy to this sort of rating.

Disclaimer: this is very much intended to be a heavy-handed, transparent metaphor for the global finance industry. The Foundation being utterly cowed by this bank is a direct metaphor for real-world regulatory bodies being similarly dominated by the institutions they’re supposed to police. Dell’Avaro joining ITCD from the Foundation is meant to be a direct metaphor for the “revolving door” phenomenon between policer and policed we see in the real world. The “oath subgroups” are meant to be a direct metaphor for real-world tax avoidance schemes and other ridiculously complicated systems set up by financial institutions. The whole of Bielefeld getting consumed is meant to be a direct metaphor for how ordinary people often bear the immense costs of mistakes made by financial institutions. Et cetera, et cetera, et cetera. Oh, and the "distinctive extradimensional reality bender" summoned with rituals are meant to be literal demons.
